macro_events.event_summary: Iran declares the Strait of Hormuz closed again after firing on a transiting vessel, threatening ~20% of global seaborne oil flows.
primary_drivers: Crude oil supply shock / risk premium spike
Tanker rate surge from rerouting and war-risk insurance
Defense spending / Middle East escalation premium

beneficiaries.rationale: Largest US integrated major with massive upstream leverage to Brent; non-Hormuz production (Permian, Guyana) becomes relatively more valuable as ME barrels get priced out. Direct EPS sensitivity to crude spike.
Pure-play US E&P with no Hormuz exposure; every $10 Brent move flows directly to cash flow. Classic first-pick on supply shocks.
Permian pure-play, zero Middle East operational risk, highest torque to WTI among large-cap E&Ps.
Frontline — VLCC tanker operator; Hormuz closure forces global rerouting, spikes day rates and war-risk premiums. Tanker equities are the cleanest trade on Strait disruption.
International Seaways — US-listed crude tanker pure-play; ton-mile demand surges when ME barrels get displaced to longer routes (US Gulf / Atlantic Basin).
Cheniere — ~20% of global LNG transits Hormuz (Qatar); closure makes US Gulf LNG exports structurally more valuable and lifts global gas prices, boosting Cheniere's marketing margins.
Lockheed — direct beneficiary of Middle East escalation; THAAD, PAC-3, and munitions replenishment orders likely to accelerate if US Navy engages to reopen the Strait.
Raytheon — Tomahawk, SM-series interceptors, and Patriot systems central to any ME kinetic scenario; Gulf allies (Saudi, UAE) are major FMS customers likely to accelerate procurement.

losers.rationale: Delta — jet fuel is ~25-30% of operating cost and is largely unhedged at major US carriers; Brent spike hits margins immediately with limited ability to pass through in a softening consumer environment.
United — heaviest international/long-haul exposure among US carriers means both higher fuel burn per ASM and potential ME route disruption (Gulf hubs). Double hit.
Southwest — historically hedged but hedge book has been reduced; domestic leisure demand most price-elastic to fare hikes needed to offset fuel. Margin squeeze.
Carnival — bunker fuel is a major cost line and several itineraries transit or visit the Gulf/Red Sea; rerouting plus fuel surcharges hit bookings. Already geopolitically sensitive name.
FedEx Express — jet fuel is largest variable cost; fuel surcharges lag, and any ME air corridor disruption adds routing cost for Asia-Europe lanes.
Dow Chemical — naphtha/ethane feedstock costs rise with crude; commodity chemicals have limited near-term price pass-through and demand is already cyclically weak.
Walmart — diesel-driven logistics cost inflation plus consumer discretionary hit from gasoline pump prices squeezes the low-end consumer that drives comps. Indirect but real.

headlines: Iran says Strait of Hormuz is closed again as vessel attempting to cross comes under gunfire

macro_events.event_summary: Iran war and $4 gasoline prices are suppressing US consumer spending, with a narrowing window to revive discretionary demand.
primary_drivers: Higher gasoline prices acting as a regressive tax on discretionary consumer spending
Geopolitical risk premium in oil from Iran conflict
Consumer confidence erosion pressuring discretionary/retail categories

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with large upstream exposure; directly benefits from sustained elevated crude prices driven by Iran war risk premium. Refining margins also widen with gasoline at $4.
Same mechanism as XOM — upstream-heavy integrated oil major captures Iran-driven crude premium; Permian + international production leveraged to sustained $80+ WTI.

Pure-play US refiner — $4 retail gasoline implies wide crack spreads; Marathon's large refining footprint and retail (Speedway spun but gas margin exposure remains) captures the consumer-facing price.
Trade-down beneficiary: when gas taxes discretionary budgets, consumers shift grocery and staples spend to Walmart. Documented pattern in 2008 and 2022 gas spikes.
Dollar store traffic historically rises when gas prices squeeze low/mid-income households; direct trade-down beneficiary with minimal discretionary exposure.
Active Iran war drives sustained munitions replenishment and missile defense demand (PAC-3, THAAD, JASSM); direct prime contractor exposure to Middle East conflict tempo.
High-beta US oil name with Permian concentration; leveraged play on sustained crude premium from Iran supply disruption risk.

losers.rationale: Cruise lines are pure discretionary leisure with heavy bunker fuel costs — double hit from weak consumer plus higher fuel. Middle East itineraries also directly disrupted.
Same mechanism as CCL — discretionary vacation spend compresses when gas hits $4 and war headlines dominate; fuel is a top-3 cost line.
Darden (Olive Garden, LongHorn) is casual dining — first category cut when gas siphons household discretionary budget; mid-income customer is most gas-sensitive.
Target skews more discretionary than Walmart (apparel, home); loses share to WMT in gas-squeeze environments — clear 2022 playbook.
Consumer electronics is deferrable big-ticket discretionary; weakest category when consumer sentiment cracks on war + gas.
Airlines face both jet fuel cost spike (correlated with crude) and leisure travel demand softening; Delta's heavy domestic leisure mix is exposed.
Las Vegas Sands — gaming/resort discretionary spend hit by weak US consumer; also Asia exposure doesn't insulate from narrative-driven selling.
Auto OEM with heavy truck/SUV mix — $4 gas historically shifts demand away from large ICE vehicles and depresses new auto demand via affordability squeeze.

headlines: Iran war, $4 gas, took all the fun out of the U.S. consumer economy. There's only so much time to bring it back

macro_events.event_summary: Report that Trump's Fed chair nominee Kevin Warsh is expected to pursue rate cuts, signaling a more dovish Fed regime ahead.
primary_drivers: Lower discount rate boosting long-duration asset valuations
Lower funding costs for rate-sensitive balance sheets (housing, regional banks, REITs)
Weaker USD expectations from dovish policy shift

beneficiaries.rationale: D.R. Horton's order book is directly tied to 30-year mortgage rates; expected lower policy rates pull mortgage rates down, improving affordability and cancellation rates. Homebuilders are the most rate-sensitive equity group.
High-multiple growth name with terminal-value-heavy valuation; directly benefits from lower discount rate on distant cash flows.
Zillow revenue tied to housing transaction volume, which reaccelerates when mortgage rates fall; direct beneficiary of rate-cut-driven housing thaw.

losers.rationale: Charles Schwab's net interest margin compresses when short rates fall; cash sweep economics deteriorate directly with Fed Funds cuts.

headlines: Trump expects his Fed chair nominee to cut interest rates. Here’s how Kevin Warsh might try to do it.

macro_events.event_summary: Post-Iran war commentary argues the US has emerged as the world's most secure energy power, implying durable advantage for US oil & gas producers and LNG exporters relative to Middle East-exposed supply.
primary_drivers: Geopolitical risk premium shifts toward Middle East crude, away from US-domiciled production
Structural demand pull for US LNG as European/Asian buyers diversify from Gulf supply
Capital reallocation toward US energy infrastructure and reserves

beneficiaries.rationale: Cheniere is the largest US LNG exporter; a narrative of US energy supremacy directly supports long-dated offtake contract demand from European and Asian utilities seeking non-Gulf supply.
Largest US integrated with Permian + Guyana growth and major LNG project (Golden Pass). Direct beneficiary of 'secure US barrel' premium versus Middle East peers.
Diamondback is a pure-play Permian producer — the most direct expression of US shale production growth thesis with no Middle East exposure.
Pure-play US onshore E&P (Permian, Eagle Ford, Bakken) — benefits from any reallocation of capital toward domestic-only production stories.
Venture Global is the second-largest US LNG exporter ramping Plaquemines and CP2; directly leveraged to the 'US as global LNG supplier' thesis.
Texas Pacific Land owns Permian royalty acreage — pure passive exposure to US shale production volume thesis with no operational risk.
Kinder Morgan's gas pipeline network feeds Gulf Coast LNG export terminals; structural beneficiary of sustained LNG export growth.

headlines: The real outcome of the Iran war: America is now the world’s most secure energy power

macro_events.event_summary: Iran asserts control over Strait of Hormuz while the US considers boarding Iran-tied vessels, escalating Middle East tensions and threatening ~20% of global seaborne oil flows.
primary_drivers: Oil supply risk premium from potential Hormuz disruption
Tanker rate spike from rerouting and war-risk insurance

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with large US/Guyana/Permian upstream production; direct beneficiary of Brent risk premium with minimal Hormuz transit exposure for its own barrels.
Pure-play E&P with heavy US onshore leverage; earnings highly sensitive to crude price spikes with no tanker transit risk.
Permian pure-play; cash flow torque to WTI spikes is among the highest in large-cap E&P.
Frontline is a large crude tanker operator; Hormuz disruption forces rerouting around Africa, spiking VLCC day rates and war-risk premiums directly to its P&L.
Scorpio Tankers — product tanker rates spike sharply on Middle East refined product flow disruption; historical playbook from 2019/2024 tanker seizures.
Lockheed makes PAC-3/THAAD missile defense systems likely deployed/replenished in a Gulf confrontation; direct order-flow exposure if kinetic escalation occurs.
Raytheon makes Patriot, SM-series, and Tomahawk systems used by US Navy assets in the Gulf; Hormuz boarding operations increase munitions consumption and replenishment orders.
Targa Resources — US midstream/NGL exporter; LNG/LPG rerouting away from Gulf loadings benefits Gulf Coast export-linked infrastructure.

losers.rationale: Delta has no fuel hedges and jet fuel is ~25% of opex; a sustained Brent spike directly compresses margins with limited ability to pass through on near-term bookings.
United similarly unhedged on fuel and heavy international route exposure (including Gulf overflight risk on India/Asia routes requiring reroutes = higher block hours).
Southwest abandoned systematic hedging; 100% domestic but still fully exposed to jet fuel price spike with thin margins.
Carnival bunker fuel costs spike with Brent; Middle East/Red Sea itinerary disruption compounds the headwind.
FedEx express air network is jet-fuel intensive; fuel surcharges lag actual cost increases by weeks.
Dow Inc uses naphtha/ethane feedstocks tied to crude; margin compression on ethylene/polyethylene chain during oil spikes that don't pass through to end products immediately.
Walmart consumer discretionary spend hit by gasoline price pass-through at the pump; historical inverse correlation between retail same-store comps and crude spikes.

headlines: Dow Jones Futures: Tehran Says Hormuz 'Under Strict Control'; U.S. Eyes Boarding Iran-Tied Ships
